hinge: A radiolucency between the roots of a molar (in the furcation area) is interradicular bone loss. Interdental bone loss is between adjacent teeth; vertical bone loss follows root angulation; horizontal bone loss follows a more even plane.

Grade II buccal furcation involvement allows probe penetration but not through-and-through. GTR (guided tissue regeneration) is indicated when there is adequate remaining bone walls and healthy soft tissue to regenerate the lost attachment. Furcation plasty is for Grade I; tunnel preparation is for Grade III.

After removing a broken bridge with carious teeth and inflamed gingiva, the first step is disease control — caries removal, scaling, and inflammation management — before definitive impressions or surgical procedures.

Inflammation limited to three adjacent teeth (#11,12,13) at the interdental areas describes localized papillary gingivitis. Localized diffuse would extend beyond papillae to attached gingiva; localized marginal would be restricted to the gingival margin.

Crown lengthening on a tooth with a short root trunk risks perforating into the furcation area during the osseous recontouring because the distance from the CEJ to the furcation entrance is minimal. This would create an iatrogenic communication that is difficult to manage.

Plasma cell gingivitis is an allergic/hypersensitivity reaction characterized by dense plasma cell infiltrate. The definitive treatment is identification and removal of the causative allergen (cinnamon, mint flavoring agents, specific toothpaste ingredients), not plaque control or surgery.

Clinically healthy gingival tissue histologically contains a mild lymphocytic infiltrate as part of the normal immunologic surveillance in the gingival sulcus. Completely absent inflammatory cells is not physiologic.

Clinically healthy gingiva microscopically contains a mild chronic inflammatory infiltrate (lymphocytes and plasma cells) as a normal host defense response to the constant microbial challenge in the sulcus. Absence of all inflammatory cells is not physiologic.

A healthy patient with a healthy periodontium who develops spacing from occlusal trauma has primary occlusal trauma — excessive forces on a normal, healthy periodontium. Secondary occlusal trauma occurs when excessive forces are applied to a reduced/compromised periodontium.

Drug-induced gingival enlargement from phenytoin is managed first by consulting the physician about possible medication substitution and implementing rigorous plaque control and scaling. Gingivectomy is reserved for persistent enlargement after the first-line interventions.

Maxillary first molar has three furcation entrances (buccal, mesiobuccal-palatal, distobuccal-palatal) with complex root concavities, making furcation access the most difficult among all teeth for instrumentation and debridement during periodontal therapy.

Maxillary first premolar has the worst furcation access due to its short root trunk and deep mesial developmental concavity. The distobuccal root of maxillary first molar also has limited access but the premolar furcation is classically taught as the most difficult.
Maxillary first premolar is classically considered the most difficult tooth to access during periodontal surgery due to its short root trunk, deep mesial concavity, and narrow furcation entrance.

An 85-year-old uncontrolled diabetic with 12 mm probing depth, localized bleeding, and 50% bone loss requires frequent supportive periodontal therapy. Three-month (every 2-month) recall intervals are appropriate for patients with active disease and systemic risk factors to maintain stability.

Dental floss selection should primarily be based on proximal contact tightness. Tight contacts require waxed or PTFE floss; wide contacts allow for wider floss types. Comfort matters but is secondary to clinical effectiveness.

Chronic periodontitis is strongly associated with the red complex bacteria — specifically Treponema denticola, Porphyromonas gingivalis, and Tannerella forsythia. Among the listed options, T. denticola is the best match. A. actinomycetemcomitans is more associated with aggressive periodontitis; S. mitis is associated with health.

Bleeding and pain around a recently placed crown likely indicates biologic width violation. Crown lengthening surgically re-establishes the proper distance between the restorative margin and the alveolar crest, resolving the chronic inflammation.

Persistent inflammation is the primary factor preventing optimal healing of the interdental papilla after periodontal surgery. Incomplete plaque control and ongoing inflammatory response in the critical interproximal zone impairs healing and tissue maturation.

Thin, flat gingival biotype makes probing depth measurement challenging because the probe tip tends to penetrate through the thin, friable junctional epithelium into the connective tissue, resulting in overestimation of true pocket depth. Thin scalloped is also delicate but less likely to be distorted by the probe.

Treatment priority should be infection and disease control first: scaling removes the bacterial etiology of gingival inflammation, then restore the carious tooth, and finally address the impacted molar. This sequence addresses the most urgent acute condition and reduces bacterial load before surgery.

Gingivitis extending from the gingival margin to the mucobuccal fold involves the attached gingiva in addition to marginal and papillary zones — this is localized diffuse gingivitis. Localized marginal is confined to the marginal zone; localized papillary is limited to interdental papillae.

The question asks about the maximum working length a curette can achieve in nonsurgical settings. Standard Gracey curettes have blade lengths around 4.75 mm, making this the maximum insertion depth for nonsurgical root planing within a pocket.

Trauma from occlusion does not increase gingival crevicular fluid flow — it primarily affects the periodontal ligament and bone, not the gingival inflammation. Smoking and hormonal changes both increase GCF flow by increasing vascular permeability and inflammatory mediators.

A periodontal abscess with visible pus drainage requires immediate incision and drainage to evacuate the purulent collection and relieve pressure. RCT would be for combined endo-perio abscess; observation is inappropriate for active purulent infection.

Necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis (NUG) is initially managed with gentle nonsurgical debridement, oral hygiene instruction, and chlorhexidine rinses. Antibiotics are reserved for systemic involvement or non-responsive cases.

Menstruation increases sex hormone levels (estrogen/progesterone) which increase gingival vascular permeability and crevicular fluid flow, and shift subgingival microbiota toward Prevotella intermedia. This manifests as increased exudate and gingival inflammation without causing permanent bone loss or tooth mobility.

Miller Class III recession (extending to MGJ with bone loss) on upper anterior teeth with thin gingival biotype is best treated with connective tissue graft to achieve root coverage and increase tissue thickness. Widman flap is for pocket elimination; apically positioned flap is for crown lengthening.

A 23-year-old with Stage III Grade C periodontitis (rapid progression, strong family history) requires aggressive initial treatment. Scaling with adjunctive systemic antibiotics (amoxicillin + metronidazole) is the evidence-based approach for this aggressive form to prevent rapid progression.

Tooth mobility of 1 mm in the buccal direction falls under Miller Class II (horizontal mobility of 1 mm or more but not greater than the crown width). Class I is barely detectable mobility; Class III includes vertical depression.

Recession confined to the gingival margin and not extending to the mucogingival junction is Miller Class I. Class II extends to or beyond the MGJ without interproximal bone loss; Class III includes interproximal bone loss; Class IV includes severe interproximal loss.
